paper_title: Effect of femtosecond-assisted vs manual descemetorhexis on the posterior corneal surface after DMEK: A fellow eye comparison.
raw_limitation_text: PurposeThis study aimed to evaluate anatomical changes in the posterior corneal surface in a cohort of patients who underwent Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) with manual descemetorhexis in one eye and femtosecond-assisted descemetorhexis in the fellow eye.MethodsIn this prospective case series study, morphological changes in the posterior corneal surface of nine patients who underwent DMEK with manual descemetorhexis in one eye and femtosecond-assisted descemetorhexis in the other eye were investigated. Main outcome measures were obtained with Pentacam (Oculus, Weltzar, Germany) and included the best-fit sphere at 4 mm in the posterior surface, asphericity coefficient Q, posterior corneal elevation, posterior simulated keratometry, pachymetry and higher order aberrations.ResultsThe differences in the posterior corneal surface were not statistically significant for any variable nor were their differences in best-corrected visual acuity, refraction or endothelial cell density. A rebubbling procedure was performed for each group.ConclusionsThe femtosecond descemetorhexis circular cut and asymmetry in the stromal depth of cut did not result in a less favorable outcome than the manual dissection profile in terms of posterior corneal surface irregularity or corneal aberration, and thus on the visual outcome.
limitations: The study was conducted on a small cohort of only nine patients, which may limit the generalizability of the findings.
research_gaps: The authors did not explore the long-term effects of the two techniques on visual outcomes beyond the immediate postoperative period.
future_work: Future studies should include a larger sample size and longer follow-up to better assess the outcomes of femtosecond-assisted descemetorhexis compared to manual descemetorhexis.
methodological_weaknesses: The study lacked a control group, which makes it difficult to draw definitive conclusions about the efficacy of the two techniques.
